segments.s304-g57: بترسند is the present-subjunctive/optative third-person plural (بـ- prefix + ترسند) meaning 'let them fear' / 'may they be afraid' or a hortatory 'they should fear.' وز ترس is a terse, poetic phrase meaning 'out of/from fear' (وز here is an archaic/poetic variant used like از). یاری کنند is the present-subjunctive/optative third-person plural of کردن meaning 'let them give help' or 'they should help'; together the clause says they should fear and, from that fear, offer help.
segments.s304-g58: نه ... کنند negates the following motives: نه از کین و از کامکاری کنند = 'not from vengeance and not from (seeking) advantage.' از here is the preposition 'from/out of,' کین means 'vengeance/hatred,' and کامکاری refers to satisfaction/fulfillment of one's desires (here 'seeking one's own gain'); کنند is the same subjunctive/optative verb form 'let them do' / 'they should act.'
segments.s304-g59: بکوشیم is first-person-plural subjunctive/imperative 'let us strive' or 'we should try.' تا marks purpose here ('so that/in order that'). پیش از آن 'before that' is literal (پیش 'before' + از 'from' + آن 'that'). کو is a poetic/archaic relative (here functioning like که/which) introducing the clause about the army; it is not the common interrogative 'where' in this usage, so no exact sense index is given from the provided list.
segments.s304-g60: بخواند is third-person-singular subjunctive/present meaning 'calls/summons' (here 'when the army summons'). The first بر with بخواند is 'against/upon' (the army calling against him). The second phrase بر بگیریم راه uses بر as a directional/privative element 'toward/on' plus بگیریم (first-person-plural subjunctive 'let us seize/take') and راه 'road/way' — together 'let us seize the road (toward him).' او is the target 'him.'
segments.s304-g61: همه باره is a compact poetic phrase meaning 'all at once' / 'in a single onslaught' (همه 'all' + باره 'occasion/instance'). دژ 'fortress' is the object, and فرود آوریم is first-person-plural subjunctive/present-form 'we bring down / we will bring down' (فرود آوردن = to bring down). The clause expresses an intention to demolish the fortress in one blow.
segments.s304-g62: همه سنگ و خاکش = 'all its stones and its dust' (همه 'all', سنگ 'stones', خاکش 'its dust'). به رود آوریم uses به + رود = 'into the river' (به here 'towards/into') and آوریم 'we bring/cast' (first-person-plural). Grammatically this continues the intention: they will cast all the fortress's stones and earth into the river.
segments.s304-g63: سپه را uses را as a topical/object marker indicating the army as the locus of the statement. کنون 'now' + روز سختی 'a hard day' + گذشت 'passed' => 'now a hard day for the army has passed.' Note the classical word order: topicalized سپه را, then temporal phrase, then گذشت 'passed.'
segments.s304-g64: همان روز 'that same day' modifies the following clause. رزم اندر آرام گشت literally 'the battle in/into calm turned' — گشت is the past tense 'became/turned,' اندر 'in/into,' آرام 'calm.' The clause means the fighting/siege was stilled on that day.
segments.s304-g81: که introduces a subordinate/clausal wish or statement here ('that' / optative). کین پدر is a genitive phrase 'the father's hatred' (in poetry the ezāfe is typically omitted). بر تو آید is a verbal phrase where the preposition بر + pronoun تو precedes the verb آید (literally 'come upon you'); آید is used in an optative/implicative sense 'may/shall come' and the following adverbial phrase به سر ('to the head') completes the idiom بر ... به سر آمدن = 'befall, come upon (one)'.
segments.s304-g82: مبادی here is an archaic/poetic optative/imperative form (classical Persian مباد/مبادا variants) expressing a wish/ban ('let there be no ...' / 'may it not be'); it governs the remainder of the phrase. به جز is the compound prepositional expression 'except (for)'; شاه و پیروزگر are the noun phrase 'the king and the victor' and are the exceptions allowed by the preceding prohibition/wish.
segments.s304-g9: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'. The noun phrase خون سر شاه ایران ('the blood of the head of the king of Iran') precedes the verb بریخت, which is a past-tense verb ('was shed/poured'); classical Persian often places the verb at the end.
segments.s304-g10: به ما بر ... ببیخت is an idiomatic phrasing meaning 'befell us' or 'came upon us'; به ما (upon us) + بر (on/upon) frames the afflicted party, and ببیخت is a past/resultive verb 'came to be/attained' describing that a condition occurred. چنین آتش کین is the thing that befell us: 'such a fire of vengeance' (چنین modifies آتش; کین is a postpositive modifier specifying the type of fire).
segments.s304-g11: شگفت آمدش is an impersonal/slightly idiomatic phrase 'it came as a wonder to him' (i.e. 'he was amazed'). کانچنان جای دید is a subordinate clause 'that he saw such a place' (کانچنان = 'such', جای = 'place', دید = past 'he saw'), so the sequence expresses 'he was amazed that he saw such a place.'
segments.s304-g12: سپهری دلارام is an adjectival + proper-name phrase ('heavenly/celestial Delaram' or an epithet forming a compound name). بر پای is a locative phrase 'at the foot/at the feet (of)' and دید is the verb 'he saw,' carrying over the subject from the previous clause.
segments.s304-g13: به رستم is the indirect object 'to Rostam.' چنین گفت means 'she said thus/so' (چنین = 'thus'). کای پهلوان is a vocative address 'O champion' (کای = vocative 'O'; note that کای can also be a title but here is vocative).
segments.s304-g14: سزد‌گر functions as a conditional/ concessive particle meaning 'if it is fitting' or 'should you deem it right'; ببینی is present/subjunctive second‑person singular 'you should see' (or 'you might see'). به روشن‌روان is the prepositional object 'the bright‑souled one' (an epithet for the woman). The structure yields 'if you think it fitting, see the bright‑souled one.'
segments.s304-g15: که introduces an indirect question/clause: 'what ...'. با ما 'to/with us' marks the affected party, جهاندار یزدان ('the world‑ruler Yazdān') is the subject, چه is the interrogative 'what', and کرد is the past verb 'did' — together forming 'what did the world‑ruler Yazdān do to us.'
segments.s304-g16: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/with/regarding' introducing the sphere or cause: 'with regard to' or 'because of.' خوبی و پیروزی اندر نبرد is the phrase specifying that sphere: 'goodness and victory in battle' (اندر = 'in/within', نبرد = 'battle'). So this segment qualifies the previous question as about goodness and victory in battle.
segments.s304-g73: "پسر بر پسر" is a repeated noun phrase meaning "son upon son / son after son" (the preposition بر here marks succession/one-on-top-of-another). "بگذرانم" is 1st-person singular present/jussive of گذراندن meaning "I cause/pass (on)"; the clause "بگذرانم به دست" literally "I pass (them) to/into the hand" uses به + دست to indicate the recipient/agential locus ("into the hand" or "by the hand"). Word order is noun phrase + verb + prepositional complement.
segments.s304-g74: "چنین" is an adverb meaning "thus/like this." "تا" is the temporal/concessive conjunction "until." "شود" is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of شدن meaning "become/come to be." "سال بر پنج شست" is a compact, somewhat archaic/metaphorical measure: سال here refers to age/years and "بر پنج شست" literally "on five thumbs" — a figurative locution meaning reaching the measure of five; the whole clause means "until his years reach five (thumbs)". The clause is temporal, modifying the preceding action.
segments.s304-g75: "به سان" is a fixed simile meaning "like/as." "درختی" is an indefinite noun "a tree." "بود" is the past (or copular) form "was." The adjective phrase "تازه برگ" (literally "fresh-leaf") follows and predicatively describes the tree; overall word order is simile + noun + copula + descriptive phrase: "he was like a tree, (one) fresh-leaved."
segments.s304-g76: Subject "دل" (the heart) is followed by the cause phrase "از کین شاهان" (lit. "from/because of the kings' vengeance"). "نترسد" is 3rd-person singular present negative of ترسیدن "does not fear." The final preposition "ز" (poetic variant of از) introduces the object of fear "مرگ" (death): "his heart, because of the kings' hatred, does not fear death." The clause links as a descriptive remark on temperament.
segments.s304-g77: "پدر بگذرد" uses بگذرد (3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of گذشتن) meaning "the father passes" or "let the father pass." "کین بماند" places کین (vengeance) as the subject of بماند (3rd-person singular "remains/lingers"). "به جای" is a prepositional phrase meaning "in place of / instead of." Together: "the father may pass, but the vengeance remains in his stead." Word order places the contrasted clause after the verb.
segments.s304-g78: This is a jussive/categorical clause: "پسر باشد" uses باشد (subjunctive/jussive of بودن) meaning "let there be / may there be a son" or "a son shall be." "این درد را" is a marked definite object (را) "this pain/sorrow." "رهنمای" is the predicate noun "guide." Word order is topical subject + jussive verb + object + predicate: "Let a son be the guide of this pain."
segments.s304-g79: "بزرگان" (the great ones/elders) is the subject. "بر او" (on/to him) uses بر as a preposition meaning "to/toward" when used with آفرین خواندند. "آفرین خواندند" is a past-tense verbal phrase literally "they pronounced/praised (with 'afarin')" — خواندند is past plural "they said/uttered." The whole means "the elders pronounced acclaim upon him."
segments.s304-g80: "ورا" (to him) is an enclitic/prepositional form meaning "to him/unto him." Following it, "خسرو پاکدین خواندند" uses خواندند (past "they called/named") with a two-word name/epithet "خسرو پاکدین" (Khosrow the pious). Word order places the indirect object (vora) before the name and verb: "they called him Khosrow Pāk-dīn."
segments.s304-g33: Literal word order places the locative/measure phrase between subject and verb: 'زمین' (subject) + 'هفت فرسنگ' (an adverbial measure of extent = 'for seven farsangs') + object 'لشکر' and the past verb 'گرفت' ('took/held'). Poetic inversion is normal: here it means the plain/ground 'held' (i.e., was occupied by or supported) an army extending seven farsangs.
segments.s304-g34: The prepositional phrase 'ز لشکر' ('because of/from the army') comes first to mark cause, followed by the personified subject 'زمین' and then the idiomatic verb phrase 'دست بر سر گرفت' ('put its hand upon its head', i.e. lamented/despaired). 'گرفت' is simple past third-person singular.
segments.s304-g35: Verb–subject order ('سراپرده زد رستم') is common in epic verse: 'Rostam struck the inner curtain.' The phrase 'از دست راست' is an instrumental expression ('with/from his right hand'), where 'از' functions like English 'with/by' and 'دست راست' means 'right hand.'
segments.s304-g36: 'ز شاه جهاندار' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from the king, the world-ruler' placed before the verb; 'لشکر' is the object ('an army') and 'بخواست' is past-tense 'he asked/desired' (here 'requested an army'). Object-before-verb order is typical in classical Persian.
segments.s304-g37: 'به چپ' ('on the left') is a locative adverbial; 'بر فریبرز' means 'facing/opposite Fereyburz' ('بر' = 'against/over' here). The copula 'بود' ('was') links to the subject 'کاووس' placed after the locative phrase—poetic inversion of normal English order.
segments.s304-g38: 'دل‌افروز' is a predicative adjective meaning 'heart-cheering/delightful.' The instruments 'با بوق و با کوس' ('with trumpet and with drum') use repeated 'با' for emphasis; 'بود' is the past copula 'was.' Together they describe Kavus's cheering presence accompanied by horn and drum.
segments.s304-g39: 'برفتند و بردند' are two past-tense verbs in sequence ('they went and they carried'), a simple narrative progression. 'پرده‌سرای' is the object ('the curtain-chamber / stage/pavilion') which they took away or removed.
segments.s304-g40: The epithet 'سیم روی' ('silver-faced') precedes the name 'گودرز' as a descriptive phrase; 'بگزید' is past tense 'he chose' and 'جای' ('place') is its object. Classical Persian allows the object after the verb—here 'Goudarz (described as silver-faced) chose a place.'
segments.s304-g49: چنین (thus) is an adverb preposing the manner of speech; گفت is the verb 'said' in past (here used narratively), and با + name functions as 'to/with' marking the addressee ('said to Rostam of Piltan'). Word order (چنین گفت با X) is idiomatic in classical Persian for 'thus he said to X.'
segments.s304-g50: که introduces a reported clause ('that'); این is the demonstrative 'this' pointing to the following noun phrase; نامور is an adjective 'renowned'; مهتر is a nominal 'chief/headman' modifying انجمن; انجمن is the head noun 'assembly/meeting' — together 'this renowned chief of the assembly.'
segments.s304-g51: چنین ('such/so') qualifies the following clause; دارم is present first-person 'I have' (possession of a hope); امید is the noun 'hope' and takes the following clause as its content. The proper subordinate marker 'که' is omitted here, so the name کافراسیاب immediately begins the content clause as its subject.
segments.s304-g52: نبیند is a negative third-person present/subjunctive form meaning 'will not / does not see' (classical usage here gives a future/optative sense with هرگز 'never'); جهان is the object 'the world' (here meaning the world/life); نیز here intensifies 'also/again' (rendered 'again' or 'anymore'); هرگز is the adverb 'never'; به خواب is a prepositional phrase 'in a dream/while asleep' (به + خواب = 'in sleep/dream').
segments.s304-g53: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'); کشته‌گر is a compound noun 'slayer/murderer'; زنده آید is a verb phrase 'comes alive' — آید is a third-person present/subjunctive/optative used here for a future/contingent event; به دست is a prepositional phrase 'into the hand(s)' (i.e., into someone's power or custody).
segments.s304-g54: ببیند is third-person present/subjunctive/optative of 'to see' (here used deictically/predictively: 'will see' or 'may see'); سر تیغ is a genitive/compound 'the head/tip of the sword' (سر governing تیغ); یزدان‌پرست follows as a postmodifier meaning 'the God‑worshipper' (i.e., 'the tip of the sword of the pious/God‑worshipper'). The chain is [سر [تیغ [یزدان‌پرست]]].
segments.s304-g55: برآنم is a fixed predicate meaning 'I am resolved/determined'; که introduces the content clause; او را uses the definite-object/topic marker را after the pronoun 'he' (here marking the beneficiary of the action); ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side' (ز = 'from', هر سو = 'every direction/side'); سپاه is the noun 'army/troops' and is the subject that will perform the coming.
segments.s304-g56: به یاری is a prepositional phrase 'to/for help' (به + یاری = 'to the aid'); بیاید is third-person present/subjunctive/optative 'may come' or 'should come' — used to express hoped-for arrival; بدین رزمگاه is a locative phrase 'to this battlefield' — بدین (ب + دین) is an archaic/poetic fused form meaning 'to this' and رزمگاه is 'battlefield/place of combat'.
segments.s304-g41: This clause is compressed, typical of epic diction: شب آمد (literally “night came” = “night fell”), followed by بر آمد + خروش (the prefix بر + آمد forms برآمد “rose/issued forth”, here governing خروش “a cry/roar”). ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase “from every side,” placed between the verb and its noun for poetic effect.
segments.s304-g42: تو گفتی is used idiomatically: though literally “you said,” in this classical passage it functions like an impersonal or proverbial comment — “one would say/you might say.” جهان را is the definite/possessive-topic marked phrase (“the world” with را); بدرید is a past tense verb “tore,” and گوش completes the object as “ear.” Word order places the possessor (جهان را) before the verb and the possessed noun (گوش).
segments.s304-g43: زمین را places the earth as the topical/possessed noun (را marks definiteness/topic). همی is an archaic adverb adding emphasis or continuity (often translated here as “indeed”/“so”), and دل برآمد means “the heart rose” — برآمد again = “arose/issued forth.” ز جای = “from its place.” The phrasing treats the earth’s heart as a poetic subject that ‘rises’ from its place.
segments.s304-g44: ز بس is a causal expression here (“from/for so much” = “because of so many”), followed by ناله = “lament/cry.” The sequence بوق و شیپور و نای lists the instruments (horn, trumpet, and ney) producing the laments; و is the coordinating “and.”
segments.s304-g45: چو introduces a temporal clause here = “when.” خورشید برداشت از چرخ uses برداشت in the sense “lifted/rising” (برداشت = “rose up”), so the phrase means “when the sun rose from the sky.” زنگ at the end is a following noun “the bell/ringing,” which picks up the action that follows.
segments.s304-g46: بدرید is a past tense verb “tore” (agent omitted here, as is common in narrative or as a passive/impersonal scene description). پیراهن مشک رنگ is a noun phrase “musk‑colored robe/shirt”; مشک رنگ is literally “musk‑color” (noun + رنگ = adjective phrase). Word order places verb before the garment for brisk poetic narration.
segments.s304-g47: نشست از بر اسب is an idiomatic classical sequence meaning “he alighted/dismounted from the horse” (نشست = sat/settled, and از بر = from off/on). شبرنگ qualifies اسب (“a variegated/dappled steed”), and شاه at the end identifies the rider (possessor after the noun, a common poetic ordering).
segments.s304-g48: بیامد is simple past “he came.” بگردید here is from گشتن with the sense “turned/ moved about” (imperf./past narrative form), and گرد سپاه = “around the host/army” (گرد = around). The whole gives the sequence: he came and the troops moved/gathered around him.
segments.s304-g25: کز here is the poetic preposition meaning "from/of whom" and introduces the source of what follows; اویست functions as an older/poetic form meaning "is from him" (the lexical senses given do not match this grammatical use, so the element is effectively the pronoun + copula). پیروزی و دستگاه are the coordinated nouns "victory and dominion/power," with دستگاه meaning court/establishment/power in this context. Word order places the source phrase before the things that proceed from it.
segments.s304-g26: هم او is an emphatic combination: هم = "also/too" and او = "he/He" (referring to the same source just named). آفریننده is a nominal agent "Creator/Maker." هور و ماه are a poetic pair meaning "light/splendor and (the) moon," joined by و "and." The phrase states another attribute of the same referent introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s304-g27: ز یک سوی is a prepositional phrase meaning "on/from one side;" سوی here means "side/direction." آن شارستان is the definite noun phrase "that city/town." The verb بود (past of بودن) comes at the end, so the clause literally reads "From one side of that town a mountain was."
segments.s304-g28: This clause begins with ز (here "from/with regard to") followed by پیکار "battle" and لشکر "army." بی + اندوه is a compound meaning "without sorrow" and بود is the past copula "was." The whole clause says the army was without sorrow in relation to the battle; the normal Persian order puts the copula at the end.
segments.s304-g29: به روی دگر is a locative phrase "on the other side/surface;" به here marks location. بودش is the older poetic form of "it was" (بود + enclitic -ش = "it was/there was it"). آب روان is a noun phrase "flowing water" (آب = water, روان = flowing). Word order: locative phrase — copula — noun phrase.
segments.s304-g30: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause referring back to the water: "which/so that." روشن شدی is a passive/resultative expression "was made bright/was illuminated" (شدی = became/was made). مرد را marks "the man" as the affected person (را = definite object/ topical marker). زو روان literally "from it, (his) soul/spirit" places the source and the affected limb/thing: the water caused the man's spirit (روان) to be made bright. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect but follows classical usage.
segments.s304-g31: کشیدند is the past plural verb "they drew/they set up/pitched." بر دشت is a locative prepositional phrase "upon the plain." پرده سرای is a compound meaning something like "curtained dwellings/tents" (پرده = curtain; سرای here denotes a dwelling/place). The verb appears first and the direct object/place phrases follow in the classical word order.
segments.s304-g32: ز هر سوی = "from every direction/side." دژ پهلوانی is a genitive/attributive grouping meaning "the fortress/citadel of heroism" (دژ = fortress; پهلوانی = heroism/valor). به پای literally "to the foot" indicates position at the base; the whole phrase conveys that heroic hosts or a fortress of heroes were positioned around/at the foot from every side. Classical Persian places these adverbial phrases after the verb.
segments.s304-g17: بدی را (badī rā) places the noun 'بدی' (evil) up front and uses the postposition 'را' as the definite/object marker/emphatic marker. کجا (kojā) is the interrogative 'where' used rhetorically here (≈ 'when/where is it right'). نام بد بر بدی is an instance of the idiom 'نام ... بر ... نهادن' or 'to place a name upon X' — literally 'to put the name “bad” upon the evil,' i.e. 'to call the evil “bad”.' Word order is poetic: object/topic first, question word, then the verb-like noun phrase 'put a name on'.
segments.s304-g18: به تندی و کژی و نابخردی uses به here in the adverbial/causal sense 'by / because of' (introducing the grounds or manner). The three nouns تندی, کژی, نابخردی are a coordinated list ('haste and crookedness and folly') giving reasons/qualities; there is no copula — they function adverbially to modify the preceding clause.
segments.s304-g19: گریزان شد is a participial adjective (گریزان, 'fleeing') plus شد (past auxiliary) yielding the sense 'fled/escaped.' از دست ما is an idiom 'from our hand' meaning 'out of our power/escape our grasp' (دست = power/control). بر حصار uses بر + place to indicate direction or goal ('onto/against the ramparts' or 'to the fortress'). The verb is placed after the participle in typical poetic order.
segments.s304-g20: بر این‌سان ('in this way/thus') sets the manner. برآسود is a compound verbal/adjectival form (بر + آسود) meaning 'was at rest/was relieved' — the auxiliary 'شد' is omitted poetically. از روزگار indicates the source/thing from which it was relieved: 'from the fortunes/fate' (روزگار used in the sense of 'fate' or the hardships of life).
segments.s304-g21: بدی کو: بدی ('evil' or 'the evil one') followed by the archaic poetic interrogative کو (short for 'کجاست' / 'where is') gives 'Where is the evil?'. بد here is an appositive/epithet 'the bad one.' آن جهان را سرست uses the construction 'سر بودن [X را]' meaning 'to be the head of X'; سرست is a contracted poetic form of 'سر است' ('is head'), and آن جهان را is the object marked by را ('of that world').
segments.s304-g22: به پیری رسیده is a perfect/adjectival phrase 'having reached old age' (به introduces the state reached). کنون is the adverb 'now.' بترست is the optative/subjunctive form with the archaic b- prefix ('بترسد' → here 'let him fear' or 'he should fear'); the verb expresses an exhortation/result directed at the subject previously mentioned.
segments.s304-g23: بدین‌گر ('if so / in that case') is a conditional/discourse marker introducing the speaker's reaction. ندارم is the first-person present 'I do not have / I have not.' ز یزدان سپاس literally 'thanks from God' means 'thanks to God' or 'gratitude toward God' — here the object is 'سپاس' (thanks), governed by 'ندارم.' The preposition-like ز is the poetic form of 'from/of.'
segments.s304-g24: مبادا is an optative/negative wish meaning 'may it not be / let it not happen.' که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...' شب زنده باشم combines شب زنده ('awake by night; keeping vigil') with باشم (subjunctive 'I be'), yielding 'that I be awake at night.' سه پاس ('three watches') is a time-measure phrase (three sentry-watches) functioning as the extent/duration — the clause expresses the speaker's wish not to be kept awake standing guard for three watches.
segments.s304-g1: دو هفته بر این‌گونه شادان بزیست is an adverbial clause of duration + manner plus a past-tense verb. دو هفته is a duration phrase 'two weeks' placed before the verb and licensed by the preposition بر (here functioning like 'for/during'); این‌گونه شادان is a manner phrase 'in this way, joyfully' modifying the verb; بزیست is the past third-person singular 'he lived' in an older/poetic form (ب‑ prefix + زیست).
segments.s304-g2: که introduces a subordinate (rhetorical) clause. داند is third-person singular ('knows') used here as a general or rhetorical present. The second که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the indirect question; فردا is temporal 'tomorrow'; دل‌افروز is a compound adjective 'heart‑cheering / delightful'; کیست is the copular interrogative 'who is'. Together the clause means 'who knows who will be the heart‑delighter tomorrow'.
segments.s304-g3: سیم هفته is an adverbial time expression 'in/at the third week' (سیم an archaic/poetic form). کیخسرو آمد is the main past clause 'Keykhosrow came'. به + گنگ is a prepositional phrase: به is the directional preposition 'to/into' and گنگ here is either a place-name or an adjective 'muffled/indistinct' used adverbially; the phrase marks where/with what quality he came (the exact nuance is poetic/ambiguous).
segments.s304-g4: شنید is past 'he heard' and takes as its object the noun phrases that follow. آن غو نای is a genitive/attributive sequence 'that uproar of the ney' (غو modifies نای), and و آوای چنگ is 'and the melody/sound of the harp' (آوا + genitive چنگ). Word order is typical Persian noun+noun possessive/attributive (modifier before head), and آن is the demonstrative 'that'.
segments.s304-g5: بخندید is past 'he laughed'. The conjunction و links this clause to the next: برگشت is past 'he returned'. گرد حصار is a circumstantial phrase 'around the fortress' where گرد functions as 'around/about' and حصار is 'fortress/wall'. The sequence is straightforward: he laughed and then returned about the fortress.
segments.s304-g6: بماند is past third-person singular 'he remained / was left' (ب‑ prefix + ماند). اندر is an older preposition meaning 'in/within'; آن گردش روزگار is a poetic compound 'that turning/circuit of time' — گردش (turn, circuit) + روزگار (time/fate). The phrase means 'he remained within that turn of fate' (i.e. stayed in the course/vicissitudes of events).
segments.s304-g7: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus said / thus spoke'. کان کو here functions as an archaic relative 'he who / the one who' (کان + کو), introducing a relative clause; چنین باره کرد is the subordinate clause 'had done such an instance/deed' with باره 'instance/occasion' and کرد past 'did'. The whole segment = 'Thus spoke he who had done such a thing.'
segments.s304-g8: نه is negation 'not'. از بهر is a compound prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of / on account of' (از used with بهر in an older locution). پیکار is 'battle / combat' and functions as the goal/reason phrase: 'not for battle'. پتیاره is an obscure/archaic noun here (lexical meaning uncertain in the edition provided) and is the object of کرد 'did/made' (کرد is past 'he did/made'). The clause therefore denies that the action was undertaken for battle.
segments.s304-g65: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” The clause بَه دیوار گیرد پناه is standard classical/poetic word order: verb (گیرد, “takes”) comes before its object (پناه, “refuge”), with the prepositional phrase به دیوار (“at/to the wall”) marking the place. This yields “when the enemy takes refuge at the wall.”
segments.s304-g66: ز here is the poetic preposition “from/of,” introducing the things not feared. نترسد is 3rd-person singular negative present (“does not fear”); in classical Persian the verb may precede or follow the subject, and here سپاه (‘the army’) follows the verb (inverted order). کینش is کین + ـش (“his/its enmity”), coordinated with پیکار by و.
segments.s304-g67: شکسته دلست is a classical predicate: شکسته (broken) modifies دل (heart), and the older copula -ست (is) attaches so that دلست = “is of heart / is hearted.” او is the third‑person subject/pronoun and follows the predicate (typical poetic order). بدین شارستان is a locative/target phrase (ب + این = “to/at this”) + شارستان (“city”), giving “in/at this city.”
segments.s304-g68: کزین پس is a temporal/causal phrase: کزین = “from/after this,” پس = “after/thereafter.” شود is the 3rd-person singular of شدن “become” (here future/resultive sense), and بی‌گمان is an adverbial modifier “without doubt.” خارستان (thorn‑bush/thicket) is the predicted result; literal order is ‘from this after becomes without‑doubt thorn‑thicket.’
segments.s304-g69: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” گفتار کاووس is a genitive phrase “the speech/words of Kavus.” یاد آوریم is a verbal phrase formed from the noun یاد + the verb آوردن (آوریم, 1st‑person plural “we bring/recall”) and functions as ‘we remember/bring to mind.’ The verb is first‑person plural, fitting the narrative speaker.
segments.s304-g70: روان را uses را as the definite/object marker (the thing affected). همه سوی is an adverbial phrase “to every side/toward all directions.” داد آوریم is a compound verb where داد (here understood as a noun/lexeme used with آوردن) and آوریم (“we bring”) together mean something like “we brought/gave” or “we rendered/brought about”; in context it expresses an action performed by the speakers on روان. Word order is object (روان را) + adverbial (همه سوی) + verb (داد آوریم), which is normal in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s304-g71: کجا گفت is a rhetorical question phrase (literally “where was it said?”), here introducing reported content. کاین is a demonstrative “this/that,” and کین is “enmity/vengeance.” با دار و برد links two items with با (“with”): دار (here ‘possessions/house/wealth’ in context) and برد (‘gain, spoils’). The clause is setting up the thing supposedly stated: “that this enmity with possession and gain…”
segments.s304-g72: بپوشد is the subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person form of پوشیدن with the prefix بـ (classical/subjunctive form) meaning “would cover/cover.” زمانه is the grammatical subject (“time/the age/the world”). به زنگار و گرد is an instrumental/with‑phrase: به + زنگار و گرد (“with rust and dust”). Together: “(who) said that time would cover (it) with rust and dust?”

sentences.s304-s29: Let them be afraid and, from that fear, give aid; not act out of hatred or from seeking their own advantage.
sentences.s304-s30: Let us strive so that, before the army summons (him), we may seize the road to him.
sentences.s304-s31: At once we will bring down the fortress; we will cast all its stones and dust into the river.
sentences.s304-s32: Now the army's hard day passed; on that same day the battle grew calm.
sentences.s304-s41: May your father's hatred fall upon you; let there be no beginning except the king and the victor.
sentences.s304-s5: When the blood of the head of the king of Iran was shed, such a fire of vengeance came upon us.
sentences.s304-s6: He was astonished to see such a place; he beheld the heavenly Delaram at his feet.
sentences.s304-s7: To Rostam she said thus: 'O champion, if you deem it fitting, see the fair‑souled one.'
sentences.s304-s8: — what did the world‑ruler Yazdān do to us concerning goodness and victory in battle?
sentences.s304-s37: I will pass son upon son into the hand like this, until the years amount to five thumbs.
sentences.s304-s38: He was like a tree with fresh leaves.
sentences.s304-s39: Though the father passes away, the vengeance remains in his place; let a son be the guide of this sorrow.
sentences.s304-s40: The elders cried out 'bravo' to him, and they called him Khosrow the pious.
sentences.s304-s17: The earth held an army for seven farsangs; because of the army the earth put its hand to its head.
sentences.s304-s18: Rostam struck the inner curtain with his right hand; he asked the world-ruling king for troops.
sentences.s304-s19: On the left, opposite Fariborz, was Kavus; (he) was delightful with trumpet and drum.
sentences.s304-s20: They went and carried off the curtain-stage; the silver-faced Goudarz chose a place.
sentences.s304-s25: Thus he said to Rostam of Piltan, that this famed chief of the assembly...
sentences.s304-s26: I have such a hope that Kafrasiyab will never again see the world, not even in a dream.
sentences.s304-s27: If the slayer should come back alive into [his] hands, the God‑worshipper will behold the tip of the sword.
sentences.s304-s28: I am resolved that troops from every side should come to his aid at this battlefield.
sentences.s304-s21: Night fell; from every side a roar rose — one would say it tore the world's ear.
sentences.s304-s22: The heart of the earth rose from its place; because of so many laments from horns, trumpets, and flutes.
sentences.s304-s23: When the sun rose from the sky, the bell rang and the musk‑colored robe was torn.
sentences.s304-s24: The king dismounted from his variegated horse; he came and the host turned about him.
sentences.s304-s13: From Him come victory and dominion; He is also the Creator of light and the moon.
sentences.s304-s14: On one side of that town there was a mountain; from the battle the army was without sorrow.
sentences.s304-s15: On the other side there was flowing water, which made the man's spirit bright.
sentences.s304-s16: They pitched curtained dwellings on the plain; from every side the fortress of heroism stood at the foot.
sentences.s304-s9: When is it proper to call something 'bad' — to label evil as bad because of haste, crookedness, and folly?
sentences.s304-s10: It fled from our hand to the fortress; thus it found respite from the vicissitudes of fate.
sentences.s304-s11: Where is that evil — the bad one who was the head of that world? Now that he has reached old age, let him fear.
sentences.s304-s12: If that is so, I have no thanks to God; may it not be that I must keep vigil at night for three watches.
sentences.s304-s1: He lived merrily like this for two weeks; who knows who will delight the heart tomorrow?
sentences.s304-s2: In the third week Keykhosrow came (to the place) Gangk; he heard that uproar — the ney’s sound and the harp’s melody.
sentences.s304-s3: He laughed and returned around the fortress; he remained within that turning of fate.
sentences.s304-s4: Thus spoke the one who had done such a deed: 'It was not for the sake of battle that he made/arranged the پتیاره.'
sentences.s304-s33: When the enemy takes refuge at the wall, the army does not fear battle nor his hatred.
sentences.s304-s34: He is broken‑hearted in this city; from this on, without doubt it will become a thorn‑bush (a wasteland).
sentences.s304-s35: When we recall Kavus’s words, we bring them to mind; we turned the soul toward every side and brought (it).
sentences.s304-s36: Who (or where is the saying that) said that this enmity, along with possessions and gain, would be covered by time with rust and dust?
